data.title: Visa Vs. Mastercard: Two Payment Giants, One Clear Favorite For Me
data.description:
Thesis

Visa Inc. (V) and Mastercard Incorporated (MA) share an extraordinarily durable duopoly built on scale, network effects, and secular digital payment growth. Even though Mastercard is growing faster and tends to innovate more, I think Visa is the better buy right now. Overall, Visa has a slight edge on margins, carries less financial baggage (a cleaner balance sheet), and costs less based on its current valuation.

A Scalable Payments Duopoly Built for Long-Term Growth

Visa and Mastercard are the undisputed “gatekeepers” in the card payments world. Two giants running a seamless system that links four key groups: the banks that issue cards, the merchants that accept them, the companies that process the transactions for merchants (acquirers), and the consumers who use the cards. It's an elegant setup, asset-light and brilliantly scalable.

This business model has minted money for years because it often generates operating margins above 60 because fixed costs don’t scale much with volume. Speaking of volume, they're propelled by unstoppable shifts like the vanishing use of cash, e-commerce's ongoing devour of retail, and money zipping across borders faster than ever. And that cash usage, it's no surprise that it's expected to fall to over 40% as this year comes to a close, pushing billions into digital channels dominated by these networks.

That broader economic boost helps explain why both companies are certainly making a lot of money right now. But the differences in how they’re performing point to distinct long-term strategies behind the scenes. Visa draws about 40% of its revenue from the U.S. (tying it tighter to American consumer whims), while Mastercard brings in 55% from international markets (although that is steadily decreasing).

Let’s look at the latest quarterlies for both companies. Revenue-wise, Mastercard went up 17% YoY compared to Visa’s 12%.

Cross-border volumes help explain the reason for the gap; there, again, Mastercard had the edge, with volumes up 15% vs. 12% for Visa. Digging for clues, I found that both in Asia and Europe there was a slight resurgence in travel, and digital adoption has been a lot faster, especially in Asia, than in the West.

Brand-wise, and this is simply my broad overview of each one, I tend to see Visa having an edge on the “trust” side due to its government ties on inclusion projects, such as digitizing aid in developing nations, whereas Mastercard, to me, is more about tech integrations and therefore the bigger innovator between the two. Overall, the small differences between the two companies make their competitive advantages even stronger.

High switching costs keep banks and partners locked in, which makes it hard to imagine a bank tearing out either company’s payment rails and replacing them with a competitor. Also keep in mind that network effects build momentum: as more users join, the networks become more valuable, which attracts even more users.

I'd say that regulations further strengthen their competitive fortress. This year's compliance overhauls, including Visa’s streamlined monitoring programs, raise the cost of entry so much that most fintech challengers, especially popular buy-now-pay-later services, choose to partner rather than compete head-on. Visa’s traction in that space is accelerating, which reinforces the point.

Circling back to the point about Mastercard being more technology-driven than its peer, a core strength shows up in its value-added services. Mastercard leads in this mix, as extras like fraud prevention tools and data insights now account for 40% of its revenue. In terms of growth, that segment saw a 22% increase through September. So by comparison, Visa offers a broader set of similar services, but they grow more slowly and make up 27% of Visa’s total revenue.

In emerging markets, they are expanding aggressively with governments in Latin America are sending public aid digitally through their payment networks, and Visa’s partnerships helped distribute assistance to millions of people last quarter. At the same time, digital wallets are growing rapidly. Mastercard’s partnership with Tencent (TCEHY) embeds payments directly into entertainment apps across China. Cryptocurrency remains a side business. But it still packs weight. Visa, for instance, processed more than $225 million in stablecoin settlements this year without really taking on full crypto exposure.

Stress-Tested by the Consumer

All this talk about strategy becomes clearest when consumer spending gets put to the test, and the recent holiday shopping season gave us that real-world proof. Overall, shoppers didn't hold back too much, they drove U.S. retail sales up 4.2% from November 1 to December 21, according to Visa’s tally, with electronics and apparel leading spending, and in-store purchases made up 73% of total sales.

Mastercard data shows a slightly lower increase of 3.9%. It also highlighted that online deals attracted more value-focused shoppers regardless of the paradox that household budgets are still tight. Another key factor to watch is that credit card balances rose in tandem. While this benefits both companies, there is, of course, the flip-side to this trend.

That said, late-stage missed payments edged up modestly the previous quarter, reaching 3.04% for credit cards (but the latest data does has it going down a bit), reminding us that growing debt levels put pressure on the lower-income folks. Still, Visa and Mastercard remain largely protected because they collect fees on transactions without taking on the credit risk that hurts card issuers when customers fall behind on payments.

That protection got a little boost in investor sentiment after the recent ATM antitrust settlement. Visa and Mastercard agreed to pay a combined $167.5 million to resolve claims that they fixed fees at non-bank ATMs, a legal fight that dated back to 2011. Visa paid $88.8 million, and Mastercard paid $78.7 million and both companies settled without admitting any wrongdoing. In the grand scheme of things, I think the outcome looks more like a PR win than a financial setback.

And that brings us back to the issue that never really goes away for Visa and Mastercard. Both companies face the same problem: regulators keep coming back. Just last week, Walmart (WMT) and several retail trade groups criticized the proposed $38 billion swipe-fee settlement as too weak and they argued that a 0.1 percentage point reduction spread over five years does not go far enough and called for steeper cuts. Their push mirrors ongoing backlash from merchants who want stronger card-routing options so they can avoid expensive, rewards-heavy credit cards.

What the Market Is Paying For Each Network

Visa trades at a forward P/E ratio of 27.8x, which is cheaper than Mastercard’s 35.2x.

This gap lines up with their different growth rates, but it also suggests Visa might be the better value (yes, they both get sector "F" grades, I'm merely going with apples to apples comps right now), considering both stocks have lagged the broader market YTD.

Looking at enterprise value to EBITDA, Visa trades at 24.5x on a trailing basis, which is below its five-year average of 25.1x.

Mastercard trades at 27x, also below its own five-year average of 30.3x. Overall, we can see that these figures show that both companies are trading at discounts compared with their historical valuations, and I’d argue that this reflects some of the investor caution toward the broader fintech sector.

Free cash flow multiples mirror much of the same. Visa trades at 23.7x forward free cash flow, while Mastercard trades at a much higher 38.1x.

Presumably the market rewards Visa for its size but penalizes its rival for the greater premium investors pay for stronger foreign exposure.

That said, that valuation gap reflects different growth expectations. Analysts expect Mastercard’s earnings per share to rise by 16.1% in 2026, compared with 12.7% for Visa.

But those higher growth expectations aren’t coming out of thin air. Mastercard’s value-added services grew 22% last quarter, helping drive projected revenue growth of 13.6% year over year.

Visa delivers steadier profit margins above 65% and generates incremental returns on capital that beat most competitors.

Mastercard, however, gains the advantage from faster growth in emerging markets and stronger exposure to foreign exchange trends, which, I'd argue, support its 20–30% valuation premium.

Both Visa and Mastercard have very strong balance sheets. Visa holds an AA- credit rating and keeps debt low at 32% of capital, while Mastercard carries a slightly lower A+ rating and much higher leverage at 71% (see previous graph).

Even so, both companies generate massive free cash flow, Visa produces more than $20 billion a year, and Mastercard brings in close to $18 billion annually.

Shareholder payouts reinforce that financial strength. Visa returned $22.8 billion to shareholders in fiscal 2025 through buybacks and dividends (see historical stats above), including a 14% dividend increase to $0.67 per quarter.

Mastercard approved a a new $14 billion share-repurchase program and matched that with its own 14% dividend increase (also see stats above).

Final Takeaway

I would rate Visa a Strong Buy and Mastercard a Buy. Mastercard is growing faster, but investors are paying a slightly higher price for that growth, and it also carries more debt. Visa currently has the best risk-reward ratio. Overall, Visa has comparable long-term payment network benefits, but marginally better profit margins, a stronger financial sheet, and a lower price multiple.

data.title: Walmart: This Is A 2024 Costco Situation
data.description:
From mid-2022 to today, Walmart Inc.'s (WMT) stock essentially moved in only one direction. Similar to Costco (COST), Walmart has now reached a point where it trades far above what I view as its fundamental floor.

For so long, investors who bet against Costco turned out to be foolish, as the company's P/E multiple continued to expand to levels beyond anybody's imagination. Eventually, though, stock performance that's entirely based on multiple expansion dries out.

Costco has been a significant underperformer over the past X, and I argue Walmart's fortunes could very well be similar from current levels.

Walmart's Remarkable Run

For the better part of a decade, Walmart was perceived as a boring, slow-growing stock that may only be relevant for low-risk portfolios that are looking to preserve capital, rather than beat the market.

Indeed, from May 2012 to May 2022, the market yielded returns twice as high as Walmart.

Then, Walmart finally turned a new leaf. After years of under-the-radar investment and strategic planning, inflation pushed price-conscious shoppers into Walmart's arms. What they found was an improved shopping experience, everyday low prices, an attractive membership plan, and by far the best online offering among traditional brick & mortar retailers.

The once-boring and slow behemoth has turned into an exciting omnichannel story, which includes recurring revenues from memberships, high-margin businesses like advertising, and a market share gains trajectory.

There's one thing you can be sure of: this market loves a physical moat business. Very quickly, bears became bulls, and Walmart's stock has soared nearly 3x since mid-2022, more than 2.3x the market.

Results Continue To Be Strong, But Not Extraordinary

Pretty much since 2022, every quarter for Walmart has been roughly the same, and that's not a bad thing. Like clockwork, marginally beating expectations, with 4%-6% revenue growth, on top of 6%-10% profit growth.

Last quarter was no different. Total revenue was $179.5 billion, up 5.8% Y/Y, or 6.0% on a constant currency basis, driven by a 27% increase in global ecommerce sales and a 17% increase in membership fee income.

Gross profit improved slightly by 2 bps to 24.2%, while adjusted operating expenses as a percentage of sales improved by 4 bps to 21.1%. Combined with revenue growth, this yielded an 8% increase in adjusted operating income.

On a segment level, Walmart U.S maintained comp sales of 4.5%, driven by a 1.8% increase in transactions and a 2.7% increase in average ticket. Total sales were up 5.1%, with another quarter of ecommerce growth above 20%. Operating income rose 6.3%.

Walmart International sales grew 10.8% to $33.5 billion, or 11.4% on constant currency, including a 26% increase in ecommerce led by India, China, and Mexico. Adjusted operating income improved 16.9% to $1.4 billion, fueled by strong advertising growth.

Sam's Club revenue was $23.6 billion, up 3.1% Y/Y. Comp sales excluding fuel prices were up 3.8%, with transactions up 3.9% and average ticket down 0.1%. E-commerce sales rose 22%, and membership income grew 7.1%. Operating income was up 5.8% to $0.7 billion.

All up, this was a typical Walmart quarter. It brought nothing new to the table, but it showcased the extreme consistency investors expected.

Building on that notion, Walmart itself hasn't changed its outlook too meaningfully. Net sales growth was raised, while the profit outlook was narrowed upwards.

Walmart Trades Far Above Its Fundamental Floor

In my view, a good framework for evaluating the attractiveness of a stock is a "Fundamental Floor" concept. In a nutshell, a fundamental floor is based on the company's earnings, growth, and quality, and seeks to answer the following question:

At what point, does the stock become a no-brainer?

For Peter Lynch, this was simply a 1x price to earnings to growth ratio, or PEG. Today, this 1x multiple is unrealistic. Most good companies trade above 1.5x, and very good companies tend to average around 2x.

At $111 per share, Walmart is far from a no-brainer stock, and far from the 2x PEG ratio many high-quality growth companies trade at.

At 42x forward earnings and 37x next year's estimates, on long-term growth expectations of 11%-12%, Walmart trades above a 3x PEG. For a company with mid-single-digit revenue growth and a commitment to low prices, there's not much upside surprises to that 11%-12% trajectory either.

Meanwhile, a retailer like Amazon (AMZN), trading at 30x earnings, with earnings growth expectations around 20%, trades at a 1.5x PEG, which is 50% lower compared to Walmart.

Is it fair to say that Walmart's fundamental floor is at least a 2x PEG, which translates into a 25x P/E? I think it very much is. In fact, this is far higher than where the stock used to trade just two years ago, as shown on the graph.

Again, I'm not saying Walmart should necessarily trade at 25 times earnings, I'm just saying that the stock could fall more than 25%, and it still won't reach a "No-Brainer Valuation."

Resemblance To 2024 Costco

The Costco valuation debate is one of the most popular ones among investors. On one hand, there are those who argue that there has to be a limit to a company's valuation, regardless of how great it is. On the other hand, there are those who say that this was true for Costco at 30x earnings, then it reached 50x, so why should they think it's not going to 60x earnings?

The issue I have with the second argument is that it's based on other investors' willingness to buy a stock at a higher price. It isn't based on the company, its results, or its growth prospects. At that point, you might as well buy Bitcoin.

For me, it's one thing if you bought a stock when it was attractive and kept holding even when the valuation got too high, perhaps out of loyalty, wanting to avoid taxes, or simply because you have a rule of never selling your winners.

There's a big difference if you bought the stock when the valuation was already too high, and you were hoping it would get even higher.

Indeed, over the past two years, investors were no longer willing to bid up Costco, and the stock turned into a laggard. While I presume those who have been holding the stock for a decade don't really care, I'm certain that those who bought at 60x earnings feel quite foolish.

Conclusion

In my mind, the best case scenario for Walmart at the current valuation is that it will maintain it. In other words, I don't see room for further multiple expansion, or more accurately, I don't see justification for further expansion. We can never know what the market will do.

This means that investors should expect, at the very best case, 10%-12% annual returns, but that comes with a huge amount of risk. For example, if Walmart's multiple contracts to 35x, that would erase two years of returns.

I was right here predicting that Walmart would reach a 40x multiple one day, but now that it surpassed it, I believe it has reached the point investors should Hold or trim, definitely not buy.

data.title: Harbour Energy: Another Acquisition
data.description:
Harbour Energy (PMOIF) is an English company that reports using American dollars. As an aside, remember that English companies typically report every six months. The last article mentioned how much progress has been made by straightening out the balance sheet from years past. It could be that there is a turnaround underway. Now the company has made its biggest bet yet by entering the Gulf of America by way of an acquisition, where a whole lot of United States production is located. If this works out, it could prove to be extremely profitable for investors.

Acquisition

The company is acquiring a privately held business for a combination of cash and common stock. The transaction is expected to keep the company as an investment grade idea. Keep in mind that few overseas ideas are investment grade. This is often a way to stay away from some of the ideas that are meant to separate investors from their money.

For starters, this gets the company into a lower tax area. Compared to some of the places in which it operates, this is a breath of fresh air.

This is in addition to an announcement of a small second acquisition from Waldorf Energy Partners and Waldorf (currently in administration) for roughly $170 million. This acquisition essentially increases the interest in properties that the company currently operates. A successful acquisition means the release of $350 million being held which essentially pays for the transaction (and then some). Note that there is also a creditor claim situation which needs to be resolved and might cost some money (but then again may not depending upon how things go). The expectation shown below is to gain control of the excess cash (for corporate purposes). This deal will close more slowly (probably in the upcoming second quarter).

Continuation Of Transactions

There is also a sale. When that is combined with the two deals previously discussed there is a cash inflow of roughly $400 million from the sale and the Waldorf deal expectations to help finance the large transaction.

The inflow of cash combined with the value of stock used represents roughly one-third of the transaction value. This is the kind of thing that good managements seem to be able to do all the time to keep the company leverage where they want it to be.

Based upon what was covered before, the summary shown above should be no surprise. The debt ratio will rise but it will remain just above 1.0 after the completion of all the transactions.

This management has in the past kept the debt ratio way below one so that if a deal like this came along, then the company could handle it while remaining investment grade. Therefore, investors may expect that it is very likely management will find a way to bring the debt ratio down yet again for more flexibility while going forward.

This is easily the largest transaction to date. It is also a complementary transaction in that the company did not have a presence in the Gulf anything close to what it will have after the transaction closes. Therefore, the accretive nature of the deal comes largely from the financing leverage.

There is also benefits here in that any United States business is likely to be taxed at a far lower rate than is the case for British business (and hence the average overall tax rate).

The Business

Currently, the company is known for its North Sea operations (though that is not the only area in which the company operates). This acquisition adds a growing business to the portfolio with a fair amount of growth potential in the future.

One of the things that is likely to lower the risk of this acquisition is that the management team in place is highly likely to be retained. This is a business that the existing management needs to learn all about before they even think of trying to actively manage the situation. But that means to expect some sort of decentralized structure to the company going forward.

Anything that would cause a "brain drain" to this critical acquisition consideration is likely high on the avoidance list.

Summary

The reasons for the acquisition are shown below:

One of the things to note for investors is the initiation of a stock buyback program. This actually helps to defend the dividend because the buyback program is easier to discontinue (because there is less blowback from discontinuing it). That potential discontinuation provides a cushion before it's time to consider cutting the dividend. In some ways, this is a very American strategy that is not often seen overseas.

This strong buy idea has a very long history of acquisitions, as shown below:

Now that the company is investment grade, it has come a very long way from the startup days about a decade or so ago. The action here still means that it is cheaper to grow through acquisitions than to grow organically (faster too).

However, this company is fast getting to the point where organic growth could become material in the future as well.

The actions here coincide with a lot of companies I follow that would seem to indicate that purchase prices for acquisitions are low. There are a fair number of companies doing exactly what this company is doing.

If an eventual sale of the company is expected, I would expect that at a time when acquisition prices are a good deal higher than is the case now. The other possibility is a combination with another undervalued firm in order to achieve more efficiency pricing of the combined company's stock when compared to future prospects. But none of this seems to be in the immediate future. However, I would consider holding a company like this until one of these "sale" type actions happens.

This company has improved tremendously compared to the situation when I first began following it. I would expect continued superior performance to continue.

Risks

This is the largest acquisition that the company has made so far. Larger acquisitions are riskier than smaller acquisitions. So there exists a significant risk of the acquisition failing to meet management expectations.

Post acquisition, this is a fairly decent sized company. However, the offshore business often involves partnerships to minimize the risk of large projects. Partnerships can fail to function properly which leads to a need to reorganize the partnership in a way that excludes one or more partners. With a large acquisition like this one, such an event can set back the future of the company.

There is some political uncertainty marked by threats of tariffs and trade wars. This bargaining needs to be replaced with something that is constructive and permanent. Otherwise, the whole situation could bring on a recession or worse.

data.title: SPXS: A -3x Inverse Leveraged Strategy On The S&P 500
data.description:
The Direxion Daily S&P 500® Bear 3X Shares ETF (SPXS) is an inverse leveraged exchange-traded fund designed to provide -3x the daily performance to the S&P 500 (SPX). SPXS is highly liquid with $372 million in net assets with an average of $250mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, making this strategy ideal for traders seeking to amplify risk. Given the heightened risks involved in trading inverse leveraged strategies, SPXS should only be traded for daily exposure as a long-term position may result in significant value decay and unsalvageable losses.

About Direxion Daily S&P 500® Bear 3X Shares ETF

SPXS was launched on November 5, 2008 by Direxion NYSE Arca Exchange. The strategy utilizes index swap positions to gain the appropriate exposure to provide -3x the daily performance over the SPX. SPXS has an expense ratio of 102bps, making the ETF somewhat more expensive than peer ProShares UltraPro Short S&P 500 ETF (SPXU). Each strategy exhibits substantial liquidity and virtually equivalent performance before fees.

Unlike the Direxion Daily S&P 500 Bull 3X Shares ETF (SPXL), which holds a combination of SPX constituents and swap derivatives, SPXS solely holds index swaps along with the necessary cash collateral.

Index swaps are designed to provide exposure to the performance of an index in exchange for a fixed rate. The two parties that participate in index swap contracts are the payer and receiver. The payer is responsible for paying the receiver the daily performance of the underlying Index. In turn, the receiver pays a fixed rate to the payer in exchange for performance exposure. Performance is settled at the end of each trading day in cash with performance reset on the following trading day.

This is reflected in the performance of SPXS, in which the ETF’s -3x exposure target can only be reflected during a single trading day; holding SPXS for longer than a single trading day can result in a compounding effect that can amplify gains or losses beyond the indicated -3x daily performance target to the SPX.

Inverse strategies present substantially greater risk over standard leveraged strategies as the market is generally expected to grow over the long run as a result of multiple factors like earnings growth and top-grading constituents. Though there may be short-term opportunities to exploit large downward moves in the SPX, the Index is generally expected to grow over time, resulting in long-term downward pressure to SPXS.

As a result of the long-term trends towards $0, SPXS has undergone a number of share splits over the years in order to sustain the share price. This makes a buy-and-hold strategy in SPXS exceptionally risky, even if a market decline is expected. Though the market may turn unexpectedly as a result of a lackluster earnings call by one of the top constituents or a worse-than-expected (or in some cases, better-than-expected) economic print, I believe the average investor and trader may experience a better, long-term outcome by generally being cautiously bullish of the market rather than attempting to time a bear market.

Investor Suitability

SPXS should only be used by experienced traders and should not be considered as a long-term investment. SPXS exhibits substantially greater risk as an inverse leveraged strategy and may result in substantial losses if misused. SPXS should only be used for daily exposure; holding the ETF longer than a single day may result in compounded returns beyond the intended -3x daily performance target as well as value decay and unsalvageable losses.

Additional Risks

There is no guarantee that SPXS will provide -3x the performance of the SPX; multiple factors may influence the price of SPXS, including trading volumes, volatility, and the settlement value of the derivatives. Investors must consider all fees and short-term capital gains taxes as part of their trading strategy; SPXS has a 102bps expense ratio and has an average bid/ask spread of 2bps. Given that SPXS should be traded daily, investors may be exposed to higher tax on capital gains. I believe traders must adhere to strict risk management policies when trading leveraged strategies and must maintain strict sell discipline.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

SPXS was designed to provide traders with -3x the daily performance of the SPX. Given the generally positive direction of the underlying Index, SPXS may trend towards $0/share over time; holding positions in inverse leveraged strategies may result in substantial, unsalvageable losses and should only be considered for daily exposure.

This article answers three main questions about SPSX:

  1. How does SPXS work to produce a -3x daily performance of SPX?
  2. What risks accompany holding SPXS?
  3. How is SPXS intended to be used?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Bank of America: The NII Trough May Be A Turning Point
data.description:
Bank of America's Potential For 2026 Looks Solid

So far, I've written only 2 articles about Bank of America Corporation (BAC) ("BoA"), and the latest one was published in February 2025 with a Buy rating. The beginning of 2025 was harsh, but BAC eventually recovered and outperformed the S&P 500 index (SPX, SP500) by a factor of almost 2, and the momentum is still looking quite strong.

I liked BAC's performance in 2025, and I think that the bank has some prospects for yet another strong year. Those prospects and catalysts that I see are likely outside today's consensus view (otherwise, BAC would be valued accordingly more richly). With that core assumption in mind, and after running an update on my previous valuation calculations, I decided to reiterate my Buy on BAC today.

Why Do I Think So?

BAC's Q3 revenue of $28.1 billion (+10.8% YoY) beat the consensus by 2.29%, according to Seeking Alpha, and it was the largest beat since mid-2023. But what really stood out in terms of the bank's financials was its operating leverage, which led to an EPS surge of almost 31% on a YoY basis. It was one of the biggest surges in the banking niche for the quarter, if we consider the market cap sizes. BAC's NII appears to have finally troughed and is now climbing rapidly, with management also guiding for continued growth into Q4 and 2026:

Despite a slight uptick in net charge-offs (to ~3.72% in November), BAC's credit quality remains robust as charge-offs actually stabilize around 3.5%. The diversified business model works well with IB fees growing by 43% YoY and reaching over $2 billion, while sales and trading revenue jumped by 12% (for the 15th time in a row). So, after the Q3 print, BAC has managed to add over 11% in its market cap so far, which looks totally justified.

Q3 was already priced in, and there's little to gain here if I were to base my thesis solely on that November data. But in addition to that, I actually see a few catalysts that may not be viewed by the general public as bullish for BAC.

First, I think the market is overlooking BAC's massive capital unlock hidden in the G-SIB (Global Systemically Important Bank) recalibration. At the Q&A section of the recently held Goldman Sachs' conference, BAC's CEO noted an anomaly that many investors may be missing now - G-SIB scores haven't been recalibrated for nominal economic growth since 2012. So, BAC's score drifted from 250 to 350 simply due to inflation and economic expansion, not increased risk-taking.

And then you had this massive nominal growth rate in the economy from '19 to now, and they didn't change any of the stuff. So what's happened is we have actually grown our GSIB number from 250 to 300 to 350. And you're saying, but guys, we're not taking any more risk relative side of the economy. So the whole thesis of it has been polluted by them not recalibrating.

Source: From Goldman's conference.

The current standards lock billions in potential capital flows and prospective shareholder returns for BAC investors (and its similarly sized peers), and if a recalibration hits next year, the bank's capacity for share repurchases will explode beyond the current pace, which may catch the Street off guard, in my opinion.

And so when that comes Basel III finalization. And so -- but that's the most beneficial thing to us. So I don't know until I see that. You see some of the outlines of it, but then they say, well, there'll be stuff over here, stuff over there. We got to get a set of rules on the table that we agree with. We do that, I think that allow us to have more excess capital, and we'll probably use that to grow into it while we take 100% of the capital or more is going out in earnings -- excuse me, in dividends and buybacks in this quarter, we'll buy back a little bit more as we go through the quarter because largely we earn more than we had in our capital plan last quarter. So we'll continue to do that.

Source: From Goldman's conference.

Another thing that forms my bullish thesis on BAC is actually similar to what I discussed a few days ago for its peer, JPMorgan Chase (JPM). The banking sector is known for its fast adoption of new technologies that can save even small fractions of their all-in costs, so AI, with all its peculiarities and applicability, is now the main topic for names like BAC and JPM. In BAC's case in particular, we know that AI is already "doing the work of 11,000 full-time equivalent employees" and that the bank is "rolling out Microsoft Copilot to 200,000 employees by year-end." If BofA keeps going further on this, their plan of achieving ~8% in commercial loan growth without adding headcount will likely create a greater operating leverage, probably to the scale we've not seen before.

For now, the market is already pricing in a 5-year CAGR for BAC's EPS of over 13%, and it's quite good for a $400-billion market cap diversified bank.

If the Q3 results serve us as a guide for what may be coming next, I think in FY2026, the YoY expansion in terms of BAC's EPS will be higher. I'd put a 2-3% on top of today's consensus growth rate, and I think the potential bullish revisions (or quarterly beats, whichever comes first) will support the current valuation multiple at 14-15x.

With all this in mind, I have a price target of

PT for 2026 = 14.5x * ($3.83*(1.1401+0.02)) = $64.43/share

Author's calculations.

These updated calculations are consistent with what I had in mind back in February (and what BAC has managed to achieve in reality).

I think BofA is able to keep beating the consensus by 3-5% in the next few quarters. Taking the mid-range of 4% and applying it to the current FY2025 EPS consensus of $3.69, I get $3.8376. For the exit P/E ratio, I take a simple average of the medium-to long-term median figures, the 5-year average, and the current TTM ratio

Source: From my February article on BAC.

My updated price target, or PT, on BAC suggests that there's an upside potential of ~14.53%, and I think we should add another 2.12% to that to get the total return potential.

That gives me about 16.65%, so I keep my "Buy" for BAC in place.

Where Can I Be Wrong?

There is some bearishness if we try to assess BAC's Q3 more critically. For example, I've already noted that the U.S. credit card net charge-offs jumped to 3.72% in November, and it's up from 3.16% in October. While the delinquencies remained flat, the general trend in charge-offs doesn't look good, considering the consumer loan book size BAC runs (~$470 billion). Assuming that the labor market softens more than the "normalization" Moynihan expected (on the call), BAC's EPS growth might stall even if the catalysts I described in my article today actually materialize. At 12x earnings and no premium on top of today's EPS estimate for FY2026, I get a price target of ~$52.4/share, which is 6.8% lower than the current stock price.

The Bottom Line

Despite some risks, which are always there when we're talking about banks, I truly believe that BAC may deliver a mid-teens total return in 2026. The GSIB recalibration is not a closed deal yet, but it's prospectively a nice bullish catalyst that can bring in even larger multiple expansion when it's realized. Plus, the current financial conditions at BAC are solid enough for letting the bank grow both organically and through AI-driven efficiency gains and possibly acquisitions. My PT for 2026 is $64.43/share, which doesn't account for the dividend. So, based on all this, I'm reaffirming my Buy rating.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: QXO: The Technology Playbook Behind Next Distribution Giant
data.description:
Company Overview

QXO, Inc. (QXO) is a roll-up and technology-enabled operator catering to the North American building products distribution industry. The company believes that it is an ~$800 billion market that is fragmented and has limited digital adoption. I believe that its strategic playbook combines aggressive M&A with an emphasis on digital/supply chain technology. This strategy helps it achieve the benefits of scale economics from a historically fragmented and low-technology sector.

Investment Thesis

I believe that QXO has a compelling investment opportunity as it continues to redefine and transform the building-products distribution industry. My investment thesis will primarily revolve around 2 significant catalysts. Firstly, I believe that the Beacon acquisition highlights the first platform deal, which QXO’s management plans to be a multi-year roll-up of the building products distribution industry. Next, its emphasis on technological/operational transformation with the help of AI and digital platforms can drive margin improvement and place it well in the low-tech sector.

In June 2025, QXO proposed to acquire all outstanding shares of GMS Inc. (GMS) for $95.20 per share in cash. While later on, The Home Depot completed the acquisition of GMS Inc., QXO’s initial proposal hints that it is active in M&As and is on the right track of industry consolidation. Talking about the industry trends, Lowe's Companies, Inc. completed its acquisition of Foundation Building Materials, while James Hardie Industries plc closed its acquisition of The AZEK® Company.

QXO specifically stated that it remains focused on executing a strategy to target $50 billion in annual revenues over the upcoming decade via accretive acquisitions and organic growth. By bringing in AI-driven forecasting, pricing analytics, and logistics automation, QXO is focused on setting a new efficiency benchmark for distributors. The acquisition of Beacon for $11 billion gives a national footprint, a deeply entrenched contractor network, and access to ~$9.76 billion in revenue. As a result, it develops a run-rate EBITDA of ~$930 million ($9.76 billion of Beacon’s revenue * 9.5% margin).

The North-American building-products distribution market is highly fragmented, with ~7,000 local distributors and a total addressable size of ~$800 billion. This reflects significant room for consolidation and scale. On the financial side, the consolidation between Beacon and QXO is expected to bring procurement leverage, as a larger scale will result in supplier discounts and decrease the COGS by 75 bps to 150 bps. Therefore, there will be an expansion of margins in the sector running on single-digit margins.

Let me explain how this 75 bps to 150 bps gross margin improvement was achieved. The experts anticipate ~2% - 5% revenue savings as a result of managed spending from the executed post-M&A procurement programs. Given Beacon’s FY 2024 reported gross margin of ~25.7% (leading to COGS of ~74.3% of revenue), a saving of around 2% on COGS is expected to result in a gross margin rise of 0.02 * 74.3% = 1.486% (or 149 bps). Therefore, a 1% saving on COGS is expected to result in 74 basis points. Expecting that QXO will capture ~1% - 2% of COGS, there should be an expected margin improvement of ~75 bps - 150 bps.

QXO’s acquisition of Beacon follows the track record of QXO’s management. The management team applied this same approach in other sectors and managed to build several multibillion-dollar companies. Some of them include XPO, Inc., which is one of the leading providers of less-than-truckload services, GXO Logistics, Inc., which is the leading pure-play contract logistics provider, among others.

Since QXO plans to leverage AI for SKU-level demand forecasting, there can be a reduction in the inventory days outstanding (DIO). Therefore, even a 10% fall (conservative estimates) can result in freeing more than $200 million in working capital on Beacon’s legacy revenue base. Its revenue in 2024 came in at ~$9.76 billion, with its inventory generally making up ~20% - 25% of annual revenue. This is based on historical working capital data from Beacon’s SEC filings. This works out to be ~$1.9 billion - $2.4 billion in inventory.

If we reduce DIO by 10%, it should imply that QXO can operate with 10% less inventory and could maintain the same sales volume. As a result, 10% of ~$2.0 billion equates to ~$200 million. The digital procurement engine emphasizes centralizing supplier data throughout acquired businesses, offering QXO with quantifiable bargaining leverage. Apart from this, I believe that route-optimization algorithms and pricing tools can help enhance delivery density and contractor retention, driving incremental EBITDA gains of 12 - 24 bps per turn of revenue. As per conservative estimates, the route optimization reduces last-mile delivery expenses by 2% - 3%.

When talking about distribution businesses, transportation expenses account for ~6% - 8% of revenue. Therefore, considering Beacon’s FY 2024 revenue of ~$9.76 billion, transportation expenses equate to between $585.6 million - $780.8 million. Now, a 2% - 3% reduction in the expenses can help deliver savings of $11.7 million - $23.4 million. This proves that route-optimization algorithms can garner 12-24 bps of incremental EBITDA margin improvement per turn of revenue.

How Is QXO Better Than its Peers?

Mr. Brad Jacobs, QXO’s chairperson, is a popular name in building and scaling numerous multi-billion-dollar logistics and distribution companies through the same strategy. This strategy focuses on identifying a fragmented industry, consolidating it through mergers/acquisitions, bringing in tech, and then finally, improving the margins. Thus, I believe that the broader market is underestimating this “Jacobs’ premium.”

QXO has been leveraging data-powered pricing optimization and route/logistics algorithms in its day-to-day activities. For instance, QXO joined hands with Roofr, which should connect QXO.com with Roofr CRM and help roofing contractors with real-time material pricing and integrated material ordering in the Roofr platform.

Also, there is a focus on overhauling the company’s pricing and analytics systems. It is deploying a digital pricing platform possessing real-time contribution-margin dashboards, ML-based demand forecasting (for inventory and routing) and has upgraded its transportation management systems.

My Unique Insights

I believe that if QXO’s management converts scale into sustained margin expansion and unlocking of working capital, its stock can provide asymmetric upside. My unique insight is that QXO’s future doesn’t depend on its next acquisition, but it relies on the capability to fetch high-return, low-capex economics from the customer base of Beacon and its balance sheet.

Until now, I can see some tangible benefits, strengthening my insights and viewpoints. Beacon’s FY 2024 adjusted EBITDA amounted to $930.2 million on revenues of $9.76 billion, resulting in an adjusted-EBITDA margin of ~9.5%. QXO’s results, which consist of legacy Beacon, demonstrate adjusted EBITDA of $204.6 million and net sales of $1.91 billion for Q2 2025, leading to an adjusted EBITDA margin of ~10.7%. For Q3 2025, its adjusted EBITDA was $301.9 million on net sales of $2.73 billion, equating to an adjusted-EBITDA margin of ~11.1%. Upon comparison, it is visible that the adjusted-EBITDA margin changed from a Beacon baseline of ~9.5% to ~10.7%-11.1% in QXO’s early post-close results. This increase is in accordance with the procurement and operational efficiencies I considered.

The following indicators further support my conviction.

Revenue on a per-contractor basis

I believe that a rise of between 10% and 12% in the metric in the time span of the next 12-18 months should support cross-sell execution. To facilitate online ordering, spend analytics, and delivery tracking, Beacon rolled out the Pro+ Platform and Digital Branch. As a result, its revenue rose to $8.43 billion in FY 2022 from $6.94 billion in FY 2020, highlighting a compounded annual growth rate of ~10.2%. Hence, it's somewhat reasonable to consider a conservative assumption of a 10%-12% rise, backed by QXO’s enhanced AI-enabled data analytics and its large contractor base following the acquisition.

Examination of DIO and SKU fill rates

Apart from the above-mentioned point, it is important to examine SKU fill rates and inventory days outstanding. Using the conservative estimates, I assumed a 10% reduction in DIO and improved fill rates. The balance sheet of Beacon demonstrates net inventories of ~$1,407.7 million as of Dec 2024. Thanks to the tech-enabled forecasting of QXO, there will be tangible working-capital efficiency and healthy FCF. This will help in saving ~$140.8 million. As a result, there will be an improvement in free cash flow, reducing the need for external financing. Collectively, this should help increase fair-value multiples and improve net debt/EBITDA.

Procurement benefits

Lastly, I believe that investors should focus on realized procurement savings. These are shown as a percentage of the cost of goods sold.

Beacon is backed by a recurring and dense contractor network. If its platform gets combined with SKU-level analytics, there will be healthy margins. The profit margins will be aided by an improvement in the cross-sell rate. Therefore, an increase will not be linear with the revenue growth. It will be convex.

Let’s get this convexity angle clear in our minds. I believe that every increase in the dollar of revenue or improvement in efficiency will lead to disproportionately higher gains in EBITDA, thanks to the tech-supported efficiencies and operating leverage. For instance, if QXO’s digital procurement systems and AI-based forecasting are applied on a full scale, slight improvements in fill rate, supplier rebates, as well as cross-sell should help profit margins. As a result, the convex curve will reflect enhancements in the execution of sales or efficiency. These should offer disproportionate gains and not linear gains.

Talking about the execution scenarios, the base case provides ~50% probability that QXO sees material operational inflection in 1.5 years. This should take place when the company sees significant cost savings due to favourable supplier deals and healthier cross-selling. The upside case assumes ~30% probability that there is only a marginal improvement, with the positives getting offset by integration uncertainties. Finally, I allocate a 20% weightage to the disappointed execution.

Valuation Insights

Beacon’s financial base was taken into consideration while analyzing QXO’s valuation. This is because it reflects the company’s initial consolidated platform after the acquisition. On the revenues of $9.76 billion, Beacon’s FY 2024 adjusted EBITDA amounted to $930 million. This led to the EBITDA margin of ~9.5%. Therefore, this should act as QXO’s run-rate EBITDA post the acquisition. Let us now understand the 3 cases:

Base case

In the base case, I assume that QXO will see 20%-35% growth in EBITDA over the next 1.5-2 years. The range is backed by procurement leverage, tapping ~75-150 bps of improvement in gross margin. Furthermore, optimization of inventory supported by AI can help in unlocking $200 million in working capital. The efficiency gains are expected to be seen with the help of digital procurement and logistics optimization. These efforts should help increase EBITDA to ~$1.12 billion-$1.26 billion. If we apply ~18x EV/EBITDA multiple, which is in accordance with tech-led distributors enabling margin expansion, this can help deliver an EV of between $20.2 billion - $22.7 billion. Finally, after making adjustments about the net debt, the equity value comes out to be $25-$30 per share.

Upside case

In the upside case, I assume that execution will be outperforming expectations. In this case, cross-sell penetration is picking up pace, while the initial benefits of the contract remain inclined towards the beginning. Here, EBITDA is expected to reach between $1.35 billion - $1.45 billion. By applying a marginally higher 19x-20x EV/EBITDA multiple (due to stronger margin visibility), this should result in an EV of between $25.7 billion-$29 billion. After adjusting net debt, the equity value range comes out to be $34 - $40 per share.

Downside case

In this case, I assume that operational improvements will cease due to the integration friction or delayed technology deployment. Therefore, the EBITDA is expected to stay near the current ~$930 million run-rate. By applying a conservative ~15x EV/EBITDA multiple (due to the execution risk), EV comes out to be ~$13.95 billion. After adjusting the net debt, the implied equity value is expected to be between $15-$17 per share. This remains consistent with the increased leverage and contracting valuation multiples.

I have assigned a probability-weighted basis in all the scenarios mentioned above to arrive at the consolidated fair value. Since there is a 20% probability in the downside scenario, the weighted value comes out to be $3.2 (20% * $16 per share). Using this same calculation, the weighted value for the base case and upside case comes out to be $13.8 per share (using $27.5 per share as the mid-point) and $11.1 per share (using $37 per share as the mid-point), respectively. Therefore, the consolidated implied fair value is ~$28 per share over the upcoming 18-24 months.

Risks to Consider

In my opinion, the integration battle remains at the granular level. The main challenge remains in the details. By this, I mean that improvements in margins are dependent on specific forecasting for each product and branch, rather than broader and company-wide averages. Small forecasting errors when applied throughout several SKUs will result in inventory write-offs or chronic stockouts. This can impact margins and contractor relationships.

Secondly, the balance sheet sequencing is of significant importance. If QXO finances its future small, bolt-on acquisitions through inflating leverage over the 4x-4.5x net debt/EBITDA range without demonstrating FCF conversion, it will materially reduce upside and enhance the tail risk.

Summing Up

To conclude, I believe that QXO is a differentiated compounder that marries the discipline of roll-up execution with the efficiency of data-driven distribution. QXO’s acquisition of Beacon Roofing provides a stable and recession-resistant base. Also, it provides predictable and strong cash flows, along with a dense customer network.

QXO’s integration strategy focuses on unlocking operational alpha instead of headline growth alone. Next, the track record of its management under Brad Jacobs provides a credible blueprint, converting scale into efficiency and earnings power. As the company uses AI to optimize forecasting, procurement, and logistics, its potential for margin expansion is being underappreciated by the broader market.

To sum everything up, I believe that QXO is a rare opportunity for investors looking to gain exposure to the traditional distribution business that is on the verge of structural digital re-rating.

data.title: Darden Restaurants: Pricing Power, Scale, And The Cost Of Beef Inflation
data.description:
Growing faster than the market, yet getting squeezed by high beef prices.

That pretty much sums up Darden Restaurants’ (DRI) quarter and, to a degree, the broader casual dining crowd that leans heavily on meat-centric menus like Texas Roadhouse (TXRH), for example.

Certainly, the much-talked-about 'Battle of the Steakhouses' that I've been covering for a little over a year here at Seeking Alpha is proving to be about who loses the least right now.

The chart tells the story.

Over the past six months, both stocks have given back part of their 2025 gains. Bloomin’ Brands (BLMN), the underdog in this battle, has slid even further, while Outback still shows no clear path to a turnaround.

But in hindsight, this one is still a winner.

Ever since I called it a 'Buy' in my August 5th market-bottom article last year, paired with Texas Roadhouse, the total return has reached 37.6%, beating the S&P’s 33.2% over the same period.

And once you really look at the Darden Restaurants thesis, even with margins getting pinched for now, mostly at LongHorn Steakhouse, the conclusion remains crystal clear:

  • Darden remains the largest full-service operator, and that kind of scale helps cushion even steep commodity cost increases. The multi-brand setup also keeps meat exposure under control, especially with Olive Garden.
  • Management has been targeting at least a 10% annual return through dividends and buybacks for some time now, and in the last twelve months they've returned $1.18 billion, or $10.25 per share, while keeping their cash position intact.
  • Growth levers are very much alive, from expanding Uber Direct delivery beyond Olive Garden and Yard House to scaling smaller brands like Cheddar’s and unlocking value at Bahama Breeze. With Debt/EBITDA near 1x, Darden’s balance sheet is in great shape, giving it room to pick up smaller, struggling brands, much like it did with Chuy’s last year.
  • They continue to invest in value LTOs like the much talked about $13.99 'Never Ending Pasta Bowl' at Olive Garden, $8.49 'Steakhouse Lunch Plate' at LongHorn and even fine-dining with the '3-Course Meal' at Ruth's Chris. These offers help keep price-sensitive guests coming through the door, while full-price menu items remain priced below inflation, a combination that likely supports market share gains, much like Texas Roadhouse has done for years.

Of course, quality isn't cheap.

At about 13.8x EV/EBITDA, the stock can look pricey at first blush, especially stacked against full-service peers like Dine Brands (DIN), Bloomin’, and Cracker Barrel (CBRL), for example. Still, Darden has always earned a premium, and today it trades at a double-digit discount to its five-year average multiple of 15.3x.

Opportunity or value trap? That’s the exact question I’m trying to answer here.

Quarter For Restaurants: What You Need to Know

I broke down the third-quarter restaurant backdrop in my BJ’s Restaurants (BJRI) write-up.

The quick takeaway is a broad slowdown, with casual dining seeing the most pressure, despite remaining one of the better-performing segments, the comparisons got tougher.

Keep in mind that most of the value promotions rolled out in the second half of the year, which helped narrow the gap between eating out and eating at home, from roughly 270 bp a year ago to about 110 bp today.

Even with that gap narrowing, traffic didn’t really follow.

Many guests are still reacting to menu prices that rose by double digits, especially looking to FY 2021 and FY 2022, while food retailers (particularly hard discounters) have been soaking up a lot of traffic this year.

Here’s the industry data, split between same-store sales, traffic, and average check by month. The slowdown is easy to spot, starting in July and stretching through the entirety of Q3 and into Q4:

Cross-referencing the data reveals that starting in September and October, The Conference Board reported a deterioration in household financial conditions, with December showing families in a bad financial situation outnumbering those in good condition.

The job market also cooled over this stretch.

Excluding any noise from government shutdowns, unemployment rose from 4.1% in June to 4.6% by November, and real wage growth faded by roughly 20 bp from July to November, settling at 0.8%.

And, interestingly enough, casual dining hasn’t picked up any significant market share since July.

Taken together, it suggests that lower-and middle-income diners are still keeping a tight grip on how often they eat out, maybe even tighter than before.

But here's the new part of the story.

Florida has slipped below the national average, and that matters because Darden has a lot of restaurants there, especially across LongHorn and Olive Garden, where Florida is the second-biggest market.

Looking at geolocation-based traffic data for Florida during Darden’s Q2 FY 2026 window, traffic fell 1% in September, rebounded to 3.8% growth in October, and then slipped another 0.4% in November.

As early as September, full-service restaurants were already lagging grocery stores by about 480 bp. Two months later, that shortfall had grown to nearly 700 bp, and in both cases, QSR remained ahead.

Just take a look at this:

Peeking at the five largest restaurant chains in Florida, the traffic picture over those same three months looks like this:

Finally, the same-store sales figures that Darden pulled out of the hat in Q2 FY 2026, you can clearly see that they outperformed not only Florida, but also the national average by a large chunk (except for fine dining, which continues to underperform).

It wasn't just Olive Garden that outperformed other Italian-American concepts, or LongHorn that tied with Texas Roadhouse in the top quartile of same-store sales for steakhouses. Other brands like Yard House, Cheddar’s, and Chuy’s, which report same-store sales together, posted 3.1% growth this quarter, also outperforming the industry.

Fine dining is still the pressure point.

Same-store sales have been sliding since Q2 FY 2023, despite bringing Ruth’s Hospitality into the portfolio.

As I said before, value promotions they ran at most of the high-end concepts (Eddie V's, The Capital Grille, and especially Ruth's Chris) helped to smooth things out.

Here’s how their same-store sales stack up against other high-end dining concepts:

Strong Demand, but Beef Costs Squeezed Margins

Starting with sales, Darden reported revenue of $3.1 billion this quarter, beating Wall St.'s expectations by at least $30 million and growing 7.3% year-over-year.

As noted earlier in the same-store sales discussion, this positive surprise came from two things:

  1. Both Olive Garden and LongHorn Steakhouse came in ahead of estimates for the quarter, with particularly strong performance in Florida, a market that had been seeing lighter traffic; and
  2. They opened 17 restaurants this quarter and are expected to exceed their previous guidance of opening between 60 and 65 restaurants.

All of this led to a tweak in both full-year same-store sales expectations and unit opening guidance.

I’ll get into the details when we walk through the updated guidance.

Here's how it grew for each brand. 'Other Business' benefited from bringing Yard House into the Uber Direct delivery program, and as that rollout expands to additional brands, there’s room for more double-digit sales growth.

But now the hard part begins for Darden.

Menu prices ran about 130 bp below inflation over the period, with November seeing just a 1.7% increase. As beef costs have recently skyrocketed, Darden has seen its margins eroded across most segments.

But before we continue, why is beef so expensive in the first place?

If you follow steakhouse stocks like I do, this is the main reason margins have been under pressure lately—and why performance has been basically flat YTD

Basically, it comes down to weak demand paired with a steady supply.

Beef is a high-ticket item that most Americans hang on to, no matter where we are in the cycle. At the same time, producers have been squeezed by high upkeep costs and droughts, leading them to liquidate cattle that would normally be reserved for breeding just to keep costs in check.

Production fell by a low- to mid-single digit just as domestic demand was growing at a mid- to high-single-digit clip.

You don’t need a PhD in economics to know how that story ends for prices!

EBITDA totaled $460.3 million, up just shy of 3% year-over-year. Even so, the EBITDA margin compressed to 14.8% versus 15.4% last year, reflecting this sharp rise in beef prices that I just told you.

That pressure brought the EBITDA margin back near its 5-year average of 14.4% and went a long way toward explaining the $0.02 EPS miss, with EPS reported at $2.08.

This wasn't a Darden-only issue either, as Texas Roadhouse also came up short on EPS.

As we move into next year, the consensus is pretty clear that beef supply will soften further compared to 2025. The USDA expects production to slide to 25.7 billion pounds, down at least 2%.

Since I expect demand to remain rock-solid (remember what I told you about the resilience of steaks), prices should remain high for some time.

Not great news for Darden, no doubt.

But when you're operating at that kind of scale, pricing protein below inflation can actually turn into a market-gaining move. I imagine that for next year the EBITDA margin should be closer to the 5-year average (14.4%) or slightly below it.

This is something I'll be incorporating into the model this time.

Buy the Bottom?

First of all, I'll show you Darden's updated guidance for the entire FY 2026:

If you look closely, they increased their sales projection from $12.9 to $13 billion to $13.1 to $13.2 billion, as well as same-store sales.

But there was more.

They bumped Capex guidance from $700 to $750 million to a higher $750 to $775 million range, as well as increasing their commodity inflation projection.

Sticking with that and assuming a 13.5% to 14.5% EBITDA margin in FY 2026, EBITDA would come in around $1.76 to $1.9 billion, below where I had it before.

To make the math easier, management guided to roughly 116.5 million shares outstanding and adjusted diluted EPS of $10.50 to $10.70. Wall St. is penciling in at $10.58.

In the simplest setup, if Darden re-rates back to its 5-year average EV/EBITDA of 15.3x and we run it through today’s capital structure, you end up with a $210 to $230 price range, with the midpoint lining up almost perfectly with the Street.

Another way to look at the stock is as a steady source of dividends, which it genuinely is. This puts it in the Gordon Model's range, don't you think?

Long term, the framework is pretty clear. EPS is expected to grow 6% to 10% a year, and with the payout ratio staying in the 50% to 60% range, dividends should grow right alongside earnings.

But let's be conservative and consider 4.5% to 5% a realistic growth rate here.

If you discount that dividend stream at around a 7.6% cost of equity, you end up with a value range of $193 to $230, which fits well with the other models we've laid out.

Given that the NPVGO assumptions are unchanged and the same EPS range still holds, I’m comfortable keeping the stock in a $214 to $222 range.

Taking the midpoints across the different scenarios and averaging them out, my price target comes in right around $215, implying upside of roughly 13.2%.

And of course, once you layer in another mid-to-high single digit return from buybacks and dividends, total return gets very close to 20%.

For me, Darden remains a solid 'Buy' and well positioned to ride out elevated steak prices for quite some time, as does Texas Roadhouse.

data.title: Motorola Solutions: Paying Up For Quality As Silvus Unlocks Growth
data.description:
Preamble

In my most recent piece, I examined the 37 articles that I had published on Seeking Alpha in an attempt to learn from them. In the process, I was able to distill my investment philosophy into a formalized approach, one which I will employ in studying the following company, Motorola Solutions, Inc. (MSI).

Moving forward, this is how I intend to analyze companies, to reach a decision that fits my world view as an investor who values risk management before returns.

Let's start.

Level 1 Analysis

Business: What is MSI's value proposition?

According to the 2024 10K (emphasis added):

Motorola Solutions' business is safety and security. Every day we work to deliver on our commitment of helping to create safer communities, safer schools, safer hospitals and safer businesses...

We are building and connecting an ecosystem of safety and security technologies to help protect people, property and places, which includes Land Mobile Radio Communications ("LMR" or "LMR Communications"), Video Security and Access Control ("Video") and Command Center...

We support police, fire and other emergency responders to help protect communities, while our base of enterprise customers, including schools, hospitals, businesses and stadiums, continues to grow as the criticality of safety and security becomes increasingly important.

A check on MSI's website confirms this. MSI's products and services address mission-critical needs in numerous industries:

MSI sells communication devices that operate under extreme conditions.

MSI offers video solutions, be it fixed or mobile (body-worn cameras, in-car video systems) to allow its clients to "respond to events with speed and decisiveness to keep [their] people and property safe."

MSI does not just sell hardware. Instead, the different hardware is connected to its Command Centre that integrates audio, visual and data feeds and uses artificial intelligence to generate a fuller insight into a situation so security personnel/team could make informed decisions with greater clarity, certainty and speed.

What are MSI's revenue streams?

According to the 2024 10K (emphasis added):

We manage our business organizationally through two segments: “Products and Systems Integration” and “Software and Services.”

Within these segments, we have three principal product lines in which we report net sales:

•LMR Communications (MCN from Q2 2025 onwards): Infrastructure, devices (two-way radio and broadband, including both for public safety and professional and commercial radio ("PCR")) and software that enable communications, inclusive of installation and integration, backed by services, to assure availability, security and resiliency;

•Video: Cameras (fixed, body-worn, in-vehicle), access control, sensors, infrastructure, video management, software and artificial intelligence ("AI")-powered analytics that help enable visibility and bring attention to what’s important; and

•Command Center: Command center solutions and software applications that unify voice, video, data and analytics from public safety agencies, enterprises and the community to create a broad informational view to help simplify workflows and improve the accuracy and speed of decisions.

Which is the larger revenue segment?

The Products and Systems Integration segment is the larger of the two. In FY 2024, this segment was responsible for 64% of MSI's total sales, bringing in $6.9 billion.

The Software and Services segment delivered $3.9 billion, 36% of MSI total sales. There are three sub-segments under Software and Services, the largest revenue contributor is the LMR Communications.

LMR Communications (MCN from Q2 2025 onwards): This part of Software and Services makes up 60% of the sales from this segment. As MSI products have multi-year and even multi-decade lifespan, the longevity affords MSI plentiful opportunities and demand for upgrades, maintenance, repairs and replacement. The services provided for the LMR Communications also include technical support, preventive maintenance, cyber security, system updates.

The next two sub-segment under Software and Services each brought in 20% of the sales from this segment.

Where Do MSI Sell To?

MSI sells its products and services mainly in the United States, but a significant amount (28% to 31%) comes from international markets.

The US market is not just the larger piece of the revenue for MSI, it is also the faster growing segment. According to the 2024 10K (emphasis added):

Net sales (in FY 2024) increased by $839 million, or 8%, compared to 2023. The 10% increase in net sales within the Products and Systems Integration segment was driven by a 13% increase in the North America region and a 3% increase in the International region. The 5% increase in the Software and Services segment was driven by a 12% increase in the North America region partially offset by a 8% decline within the International region.

More about the "8% decline within the International region" in the Inversion Test segment later.

Can this business survive in an A.I. age?

Deploying artificial intelligence meaningfully in its products and services is key to MSI's competitive edge, and the company seems to be doing that.

At the Barclays 23rd Annual Global Technology Conference, CFO Jason Winkler described the benefits of AI integration in APX NEXT, the many different ways Ai Assistance ViQi help users to keep their eyes up and stay focus on their tasks, assimilate different data sources to assist in report writing, provide information upon just voice commands (watch the videos of ViQi in action). This AI assist feature - and more - is not in some promised future; it is here and is available.

CEO Gregory Brown expressed confidence at the Q3 earnings call that,

We've made significant investments to integrate these new technologies in AI into our solutions and I anticipate this being a growth driver for the company for years to come.

Take, for instance, MSI's APX NEXT. CEO Brown expects 200,000 users to be subscribed to APX NEXT applications by the end of this year, and is projecting that number to reach 300,000 by the end of 2026.

Bearing in mind MSI an installed base of about 2 million first responders in the U.S., the growth opportunities afforded by just from APX NEXT is significant.

In short, the company will survive in an A.I. age; it continually invests in R&D and acquire companies that can value-add its eco-system.

Is The Business Doing Well?

Although MSI is a 97-year-old company, the MSI we know today has evolved. Referring to the 2010 10K, MSI was still selling handphones and set-top boxes for digital video, among many other things.

Gregory Brown was promoted to the CEO position in 2008 while the business was losing money. Under his leadership, in he split the company into Motorola Mobility (the cellphone side of the company) and Motorola Solutions, which he continue to lead as CEO till today, laser focused on serving governments (public safety and first responders) and enterprise customers (hospitality, retail, etc.).

Since the split, MSI has been growing revenue steadily, from $8.8 billion in FY 2012 to $10.8 billion by FY 2024. Honestly, that kind of growth is not impressive but that is a sign of a mature business that is still able to grow market share. It also signals persistent market demands for MSI's products and services, in spite of competition from other companies. Regardless of economic cycles, governments will still spend on public safety.

What is impressive is MSI net income growth, almost doubling from $0.878 billion to $1.577 billion in the same time period.

The main reason for this is the company's healthy and expanding margins. From 2012 to 2024, operating margins increased from 14.84% to 24.91%, while net margins expanded from 10.09% to 14.58%.

Another reason for the expanding margins would be the deliberate emphasis to generate more revenue from Software and Services. In 2018, the Software and Services segment was responsible for $2.2 billion, or 31%, of the company's total sales. By 2024, that has increased to 36% of total sales. While Products and Systems Integration segment grew by 35% from FY 2018 to FY 2024, the Software and Services segment grew by a massive 77%.

On a Q-on-Q basis (see Q3 2025 slides), MSI did well on most fronts as well, posting revenue growth of 6% in North America and 13% internationally, and revenue growth of 6% in Products and Systems Integration and 11% in Software and Services.

The -1.18% decline in Software & Services in FY 2024 is concerning, but as of Q3 2025 has rebounded to +11% growth, suggesting it may have been temporary (declining revenue from Ukraine, exiting business from UK, FX headwinds). The strong, +12.65%, 2024 YoY growth in Products and Systems Integration will help to drive more MCN Software and Services moving forward.

Concluding Level 1 Analysis

Motorola Solutions is a high-quality, mission-critical, margin-expanding business with demonstratively strong demand. MSI has a working AI integration strategy that is winning customers. It is not a fast grower, but a reliable compounder.

As MSI passed my Level 1 Analysis, I shall proceed to Level 2 Analysis. For that, I shall be using the P.R.I.M.E. Protocol, developed after my reflective piece.

Prevent: PASSED

At this stage, I am looking for events that can result in catastrophic losses for the company. Preventing myself from investing in companies facing high probability threats is a key part of risk management.

Inversion Test

This inversion filter draws from the wisdom of Charlie Munger. To pass this, a company must not face any existential crisis stemming from a single point of failure.

Remember the "8% decline within the International region" that was mentioned earlier? In 2022, UK’s Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) imposed a price cap on its Airwave network for emergency services to ensure a fairer deal for UK emergency services, intending to reduce costs by nearly $248 million per year.

In spite of this price cap, MSI continue to grow revenue through 2023 and 2024. MSI is not immune to government interventions but they have yet to break it.

MSI passes the Inversion Test.

Diversification Risk

MSI's revenue is spread across more than 100,000 public safety and enterprise customers in over 100 countries.

Yes, most of the revenue is concentrated in North America. Its customers ranged from the U.S. Navy to state level governments, signing both annual and multi-year contracts, giving MSI quantifiable order backlogs and revenue visibility. At the Q3 2025 conference call, CEO Brown described the nature of the contracts in this way,

In addition, we received 3 large orders during the quarter for P25 system upgrades to our new D-Series infrastructure, a $110 million order from the State of Colorado, an $84 million order from the Tennessee Department of Safety and an $82 million order from a U.S. state and local customer. These large multiyear orders are further testament to our customers' commitment to investing in our next-generation LMR infrastructure, and we have a large funnel of opportunities over the next several years.

Public safety is a necessity that cannot be simply terminated due to budget constraints.

Other than governments, MSI serves enterprise customers from multiple sectors, so there is no industry concentration risk.

MSI passes the Diversification Risk Test.

Regulatory Risk

MSI has not faced regulatory issues with the U.S. government, its largest customers. Plus, I hate to repeat myself, but the mission critical nature of MSI products and services mean its clients cannot simply terminate their contracts with MSI on a whim.

MSI passes the Regulatory Risk Test.

Competitive Moat Erosion and Technological Obsolescence Test:

Once MSI gets its sticky products into organizations, like its Command Centre solutions and software applications, switching cost is high.

Once investors understand the nature of many of the contracts, the moat is apparent. Through the way CFO Winkler described revenue recognition in the Q1 2023 conference call, it is clear that once a client is won over, it is difficult for competitors to wrestle that customer away. When a client is inside MSI's ecosystem, opportunities to upsell additional services and products continue to widen the moat,

... a $340 million federal IDIQ award to combine existing services and provide new LMR services to the U.S. Air Force, which has long been an important customer of ours.

Given the nature of this 10-year agreement, we recorded only $11 million of backlog in the first quarter and expect backlog to be recorded ratably over time for the remainder of the contract. Additionally, we expect new services included it to contribute an incremental $60 million of revenue over the 10-year period when compared to the previous service agreements.

MSI also built inflation escalators into its contracts. Q1 2024 earnings call CEO Brown explained how MSI improved gross margins,

... we have contracts that have appropriate cost of living mechanisms built into multiyear services contracts. And as they come due we will be disciplined in making sure we're recovering appropriate cost increases that come and hit the firm.

AI is a gain for MSI, and not just a productivity gain. The company has been spending aggressively on R&D, increasing its expenditure by more than 33% from FY 2020 to FY 2024, which increased at a higher rate compared to the 24% in the earlier 5-year period from 2016 to 2020. Product enhancements like AI Assist are gaining momentum.

MSI passes the Competitive Moat Erosion and Technological Obsolescence Test.

Resilience: PASSED

This is the financial fortress and margin stability test.

Although MSI has a significant amount of debt - and is not the negative net debt type company that gives me immediate peace of mind from solvency risk - its robust free cash flow that exceeds annual capital expenditure needs by 8.3 times. In a stress test scenario where operating cash flow declines drastically, MSI still has sufficient cash on hand to sustain another 3.5 years of CAPEX. Finally, with almost a billion dollars in cash, plus another $2.25 billion in its untapped revolving facility, MSI's ability to do bolt-on acquisitions is not constrained by liquidity, but more of management's consideration of its leverage ratio, and maintaining its investment grade credit rating.

For a company with strong free cash flow that amply covers its CAPEX, and a liquidity buffer of $894 million in cash and $2.25 billion in untapped revolving credit, I am not concerned, though I will keep an eye on the trend if current ratio edges towards 1.

As mentioned in an earlier segment, MSI is expanding margins meaningfully, evidence of a business that has done many things right - improving product mix in the shift towards higher margin Software and Services, pricing power, price escalators built into the contracts, and better monetization of its 2 million first responder installed base. Case in point will be the customers that purchased APX NEXT. Many add on a $300 annual subscription per device to get a number of mission-critical features, essentially converting an once-off hardware sale into a sticky, recurring, high-margin revenue stream.

This further reinforces MSI's moat characteristic.

CFO Winkler stated at the Q4 2024 conference call that MSI had paid 14 years of consecutive, double-digit increase. This is great news for dividend growth investors provided such increases are sustainable. MSI's conservative 30.6% FCF payout ratio, supported by a FCF growth rate that exceeds dividend growth rate, further signals to me that management is exercising a conservative capital allocation policy while decisively rewarding shareholders.

What I will keep in mind would be the declining dividend growth rate compared to its historical dividend growth rate. This is not a concern in terms of dividend sustainability; in fact it is a good thing as no one should expect that double-digit dividend growth rate to continue in perpetuity, and it shows that management is allocating capital thoughtfully.

MSI passes the Resilience Test.
